names.japanese_name: パラオキシ安息香酸メチル
names.japanese_label_name: パラオキシ安息香酸メチル

natural_occurrence.description: A synthetic ester of p-hydroxybenzoic acid. The most commonly used member of the paraben family, with broad-spectrum antimicrobial and antifungal activity. Widely used in cosmetics and pharmaceuticals; limited but approved use in food products.

manufacturing.description: Produced by esterification of p-hydroxybenzoic acid with methanol in the presence of an acid catalyst (typically sulfuric acid or hydrochloric acid). Industrial production uses continuous esterification processes followed by purification through crystallization.

other_uses.cosmetics: Most widely used preservative in cosmetics, personal care products, shampoos, and lotions (up to 0.4%)
other_uses.industrial: Used in industrial products and coatings
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Extensively used in pharmaceutical preparations, oral medications, and topical products

eu.conditions: Group ADI of 0-10 mg/kg bw/day for methyl and ethyl parabens; permitted as conditionally permitted preservative in limited food categories; cosmetics limited to 0.4% (or 0.8% for mixed parabens)
eu.legal_reference: Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008, Annex II; EC Directive 95/2/EC

usa.notes: FDA recognizes as GRAS for food and cosmetic antibacterial preservation

japan.japanese_regulation_name: パラオキシ安息香酸メチル
japan.notes: Part of paraben group (5 types designated); used with usage standards

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0–10 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for methyl and ethyl parabens)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 10 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for methyl and ethyl parabens and their sodium salts)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA establishes group ADI of 10 mg/kg bw/day for methyl and ethyl parabens and their sodium salts
EU bans propylparaben (E216) from food; methyl and ethyl parabens remain approved
EU bans isopropylparaben from all uses (cosmetics and food)
European Commission classifies all parabens as Category 1 endocrine disrupters; safety opinion under review

commonly_found_in: Cosmetics and personal care products (primary use - over 90% of applications)
Limited use in beverages and processed foods

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA/EFSA group ADI for methyl and ethyl parabens. CONTROVERSY: Methylparaben shows estrogenic effects in animal studies and has been classified as a Category 1 endocrine disrupter by the EU. However, 2008 study found NO competitive binding for human estrogen/androgen receptors for methylparaben (unlike longer-chain parabens). Considered safer than propyl/butyl parabens. EU SCCS (2023) states safe at up to 0.4% in cosmetics. Declining use in food; primarily used in cosmetics where dermal absorption is limited.
